sets.passage:
[A] When engineers first dreamed of thinking machines, the conversation was largely technical: could a computer reason at all? Today the harder questions are moral rather than mechanical. As algorithms increasingly decide who receives a loan, which patients are prioritised, and even how long a prisoner is detained, society is forced to ask whether such consequential judgements should ever be delegated to systems we only partly understand.
[B] A recurring worry concerns the hidden prejudice that automated systems can quietly inherit. Because most models learn by digesting enormous archives of past human decisions, they tend to reproduce the unfairness embedded in that history. A recruitment tool trained on decades of records may, without any malicious instruction, come to favour the kind of applicant who was favoured before, perpetuating disadvantage under a veneer of mathematical neutrality.
[C] Compounding the problem is the difficulty of explanation. The most powerful contemporary models reach conclusions through millions of internal adjustments that resist any tidy summary. When a person is refused insurance, neither the applicant nor, often, the company can give a clear reason. This opacity collides with a basic expectation of justice: that those affected by a ruling deserve to be told why it was made.
[D] Closely tied to explanation is the thorny matter of blame. If a self-navigating vehicle injures a pedestrian, who should answer for it: the firm that built the software, the owner who switched it on, or the engineers who trained it months earlier? Existing law assumes a human agent behind every harmful act, and that assumption strains badly once decisions are dispersed across designers, data, and machines.
[E] Some thinkers insist the remedy lies in keeping a human firmly in command. On this view, machines should advise but never finally decide, leaving a responsible person to ratify or overturn each recommendation. Critics counter that humans tend to defer lazily to the screen, rubber-stamping outputs they neither grasp nor question, so that supervision becomes a comforting fiction rather than a genuine safeguard.
[F] Privacy supplies yet another arena of unease. Sophisticated systems are hungry for data, and the appetite encourages relentless collection of personal information, from shopping habits to facial features. Even apparently harmless fragments, once combined, can expose intimate details a person never meant to reveal, turning ordinary convenience into a quiet erosion of the right to be left alone.
[G] Against this catalogue of dangers, optimists urge a sense of proportion. The same technologies that threaten fairness can also detect tumours earlier than any physician, translate between strangers instantly, and warn of failing machinery before disaster strikes. To reject the tools wholesale, they argue, would be to forfeit immense benefits out of an exaggerated fear of misuse.
[H] The emerging consensus is therefore neither blind enthusiasm nor flat refusal, but disciplined governance. Sensible rules, independent auditing, and transparent design can let communities harvest the gains while curbing the harms. What matters, ultimately, is that human values steer the technology, rather than the technology silently reshaping the values by which we live.
statements: Combining separate, seemingly trivial pieces of data can uncover sensitive facts a person intended to keep secret.
Because they learn from records of earlier human choices, automated systems often carry forward existing patterns of discrimination.
The central dilemmas surrounding intelligent machines have shifted from questions of capability to questions of right and wrong.
Supporters argue that abandoning these tools entirely would mean sacrificing enormous advantages for fear of rare abuses.
Traditional legal frameworks struggle to assign responsibility when a harmful outcome results from many contributors rather than one person.
A widely shared position favours careful regulation that preserves the benefits while limiting the dangers.
Opponents of human oversight note that people often approve machine outputs without真正 scrutiny, making supervision merely symbolic.
Many leading systems cannot offer a comprehensible account of how they arrived at a given verdict.
Letting algorithms settle weighty matters such as detention or medical priority raises the issue of trusting systems we barely comprehend.
One proposed safeguard is to ensure that machines only recommend while a person retains the final say.
sets.explain_zh: 1. [F] 段：看似无害的零散数据组合后可暴露隐私。2. [B] 段：系统从历史人类决策中学习，因而延续既有歧视。3. [A] 段：关于智能机器的核心难题已从“能否”转向“是否道德”。4. [G] 段：支持者认为因罕见滥用而全盘否定工具会丧失巨大收益。5. [D] 段：当伤害由多方共同造成时，传统法律难以归责。6. [H] 段：主流立场主张审慎监管，既保收益又限危害。7. [E] 段：反对者指出人们常不加审查地批准机器输出，使监督流于形式。8. [C] 段：多数强力系统无法清楚解释其结论由来。9. [A] 段：将拘押、医疗优先等重大事项交给算法，引出信任问题。10. [E] 段：一种保障是机器只建议、由人保留最终决定权。

sets.passage:
[A] For most of the twentieth century the purpose of a university seemed settled: a young adult left home, spent three or four uninterrupted years on a leafy campus, and emerged with a degree that quietly guaranteed a comfortable career. That tidy arrangement is now fraying, and institutions everywhere are being pressed to reconsider what they are for and whom they serve.
[B] One powerful force behind the upheaval is cost. In many countries tuition has climbed far faster than household incomes, loading graduates with debts that take decades to clear. Families increasingly demand evidence that such an outlay will repay itself, and a vague promise of personal enrichment no longer satisfies parents who have remortgaged a home to fund a child's studies.
[C] Digital delivery has further unsettled the old model. Lectures once confined to a single hall can now reach millions through a screen, and a learner in a remote village may follow the same course as a student seated in an elite lecture theatre. Whether watching a recording can truly match the friction and spontaneity of a shared classroom remains fiercely contested among educators.
[D] Meanwhile the labour market is mutating so quickly that knowledge acquired at twenty may be obsolete by forty. Employers complain that fresh graduates arrive fluent in theory yet helpless before practical tasks, while whole professions vanish and unfamiliar ones appear within a single working life. The notion of education as a one-off event, completed in youth, looks increasingly untenable.
[E] In response, the boundary between studying and working is dissolving. Programmes that weave paid placements into the curriculum, or that let employees study part-time while remaining in post, are multiplying. Advocates contend that such blending produces graduates who are both reflective and capable, though sceptics fear it may reduce higher learning to mere job training.
[F] These pressures also threaten the broader mission universities have long claimed. Beyond equipping people for employment, they have professed to cultivate curiosity, sharpen judgement, and sustain the disinterested pursuit of knowledge. Defenders warn that if every subject must justify itself by salary, the disciplines that enrich civic and intellectual life will wither for want of measurable payoff.
[G] Geography matters too, for learning has gone global. Students cross continents in record numbers, campuses open foreign branches, and research collaborations span a dozen time zones. This mobility spreads ideas and revenue, yet it also exposes institutions to political tension and to the risk that prestige, rather than scholarship, becomes the true currency of exchange.
[H] What emerges from these crosscurrents is not a single forecast but a spectrum of plausible futures. The university may splinter into many forms, some intimate and residential, others vast and virtual, each suited to a different need. Its survival, most observers agree, will depend less on defending old habits than on rediscovering, for a transformed world, why such places ever mattered.
statements: Rapidly shifting employment means skills learned in early adulthood may be outdated long before retirement.
Rising fees have left graduates burdened with loans that take many years to repay.
The familiar pattern of a fixed residential degree leading reliably to a good job is breaking down.
Critics worry that judging every subject by future earnings will starve disciplines of lasting cultural value.
Online transmission lets a single lecture reach huge numbers far beyond one room.
Institutions are likely to diversify into several coexisting models rather than converge on one form.
Increasingly, study and employment overlap through placements and part-time schemes for working people.
Parents who have made large financial sacrifices now insist on proof that a degree pays off.
Cross-border movement of students and campuses spreads ideas but invites political friction.
Some doubt that a recorded session can reproduce the lively exchange of a physical classroom.
sets.explain_zh: 1. [D] 段：就业快速变化，年轻时所学到中年可能过时。2. [B] 段：学费上涨使毕业生背负多年才能还清的贷款。3. [A] 段：固定住校学位换好工作的旧模式正在瓦解。4. [F] 段：批评者担心以收入衡量一切学科会使有文化价值者枯萎。5. [C] 段：在线传播使一堂课能触及一个教室之外的大量人群。6. [H] 段：高校更可能分化为多种并存形态而非趋同。7. [E] 段：实习与在职兼读使学习与就业日益交织。8. [B] 段：作出巨大经济牺牲的家长要求学位回报的证据。9. [G] 段：学生与校区跨境流动传播思想却引来政治摩擦。10. [C] 段：有人怀疑录播能否再现实体课堂的活泼互动。

sets.passage:
[A] Beneath the obvious charm of a teeming coral reef or a humming meadow lies a quieter truth that scientists have laboured to explain: the sheer variety of living things is not a decorative luxury but a working condition for life itself. Biodiversity, the term that bundles together the millions of species and the relationships among them, underpins systems on which every human depends, often invisibly.
[B] Consider the ordinary act of eating. A startling share of the crops that feed the planet rely on animals to carry pollen from flower to flower, and most of that labour is performed by insects whose names few people know. Should these obscure workers dwindle, harvests would falter and the cost of staple foods would climb, a chain of consequences reaching far beyond the field.
[C] Variety also lends an ecosystem its resilience. A grassland composed of many species can absorb a drought or a plague far better than a single-crop expanse, because where one plant fails another may endure and hold the soil together. Diversity thus functions as a kind of insurance, spreading risk so that a single shock cannot topple the whole arrangement.
[D] Medicine offers a further, often overlooked, debt. A remarkable proportion of the drugs in modern pharmacies trace their origins to compounds first discovered in wild plants, fungi, or marine creatures. Each species lost before it is studied may carry away an undiscovered remedy, closing a door on cures we will never know we missed.
[E] There is, moreover, a cultural dimension that ledgers seldom capture. For countless communities a particular forest, river, or animal is woven into language, ritual, and identity in ways no substitute can replace. When such living things disappear, a people may lose not merely a resource but a part of who they understand themselves to be.
[F] Why, then, does loss continue to accelerate? The pressures are familiar: forests cleared for farmland, rivers dammed or poisoned, a warming atmosphere that shifts the ranges species need to survive. Crucially, the damage tends to fall on those least able to register in markets, so the true bill is paid by future generations rather than present accounts.
[G] Encouragingly, decline is not inevitable. Protected reserves, the careful restoration of wetlands, and the revival of traditional stewardship have all coaxed battered populations back toward health. Such recoveries show that, given room and time, nature possesses a stubborn capacity to mend itself when human pressure is eased.
[H] The deeper lesson is one of connection. No species, including our own, stands apart from the web that sustains it; to weaken the strands is ultimately to imperil ourselves. Recognising this dependence, rather than treating nature as a warehouse to be emptied, may be the single most important shift the coming century demands.
statements: Many medicines originated from substances first found in wild organisms, so losing a species may erase an unknown cure.
A large fraction of food crops depend on insects to move pollen between flowers.
The full cost of destruction is largely borne by later generations because it escapes present-day markets.
The diversity of living things is a functional requirement for life, not merely an attractive extra.
Damaged populations can rebound when reserves, restoration, and traditional care relieve human pressure.
A community may lose part of its very identity when a creature bound to its rituals vanishes.
An ecosystem with many species withstands shocks like drought better than a single-crop one.
The ultimate insight is that no species stands outside the web that sustains it.
Habitat clearance, polluted rivers, and a warming climate are driving the continuing decline.
Variety acts like insurance by spreading risk so one disturbance cannot collapse the whole system.
sets.explain_zh: 1. [D] 段：许多药物源自野生生物中的物质，灭绝某物种可能抹去未知良药。2. [B] 段：大量粮食作物依赖昆虫传粉。3. [F] 段：破坏的真实代价因逃出当下市场而主要由后代承担。4. [A] 段：生物多样性是生命的功能性必需，而非装饰性奢侈。5. [G] 段：当保护区、修复与传统照管减轻压力时，受损种群可回升。6. [E] 段：当与其仪式相连的生物消失，群体可能失去部分自我认同。7. [C] 段：多物种生态系统比单一作物更能抵御干旱等冲击。8. [H] 段：终极洞见是没有物种能置身于支撑它的网络之外。9. [F] 段：栖地清除、河流污染与气候变暖驱动持续衰退。10. [C] 段：多样性如同保险，分散风险使单一扰动无法令整体崩溃。

sets.passage:
[A] Walk into any supermarket and you enter a laboratory of human impulse. Every shelf height, every burst of colour, every drift of background music has been arranged with a single aim: to nudge the wandering mind toward a purchase it had not planned. The study of why we buy what we buy has quietly become one of the most lucrative branches of applied psychology.
[B] A foundational insight is that shoppers rarely calculate. Faced with thousands of options and limited attention, the mind leans on mental shortcuts, judging a wine by the elegance of its label or a gadget by the confidence of its advertisement. These rules of thumb usually serve us well, yet they also leave a reliable opening for those who know how to exploit them.
[C] Emotion, not arithmetic, often seals the deal. Brands invest fortunes not in listing features but in attaching a feeling to a product, so that a fizzy drink comes to signify friendship or a watch to whisper success. When the cash register rings, the buyer is frequently paying for a mood or an identity rather than for the object in the bag.
[D] Context bends judgement in startling ways. The very same coat seems a bargain beside a pricier rival and an extravagance beside a cheaper one, though nothing about the coat has changed. Sellers exploit this by surrounding what they wish to move with carefully chosen companions, steering the eye toward the option they prefer we choose.
[E] Scarcity exerts a peculiar grip. The phrase only three left or a countdown ticking on a screen can transform mild interest into urgent desire, for the fear of missing out overrides calm reflection. What feels like a free decision is, in such moments, partly manufactured by the suggestion that the chance is about to vanish.
[F] Other people supply a further lever. Uncertain shoppers look to the crowd, trusting that a heavily reviewed restaurant or a best-selling book must be worth its price. This reliance on social proof can be benign, but it can also be staged, with fabricated praise nudging the hesitant toward a verdict that was never independently theirs.
[G] The digital marketplace has sharpened every one of these tactics. Online sellers observe each click, lingering glance, and abandoned cart, then tailor prices and prompts to the individual in real time. Persuasion that once relied on broad guesses about the average customer can now be aimed, unsettlingly, at the particular person behind the screen.
[H] None of this means consumers are helpless puppets. Awareness of the mechanisms at work is itself a defence, and a shopper who pauses to ask why a craving has suddenly arisen reclaims a measure of control. The market will always seek to persuade; the informed mind can at least decide, more often, on its own terms.
statements: Online retailers track individual behaviour and adjust prices or messages to each specific person.
Overwhelmed by choices, buyers rely on mental shortcuts that can be deliberately exploited.
The whole retail environment, from shelf layout to music, is engineered to prompt unplanned buying.
Simply understanding these techniques gives the shopper some power to resist them.
An identical item appears cheap or costly purely according to what surrounds it.
Uncertain customers trust the crowd, a tendency that can be faked with invented reviews.
Purchases are frequently driven by the feeling attached to a brand rather than its features.
Suggesting that stock is nearly gone turns mild interest into urgent craving.
Studying purchasing motives has become a highly profitable area of applied psychology.
Companies pay heavily to link a product with an emotion such as friendship or success.
sets.explain_zh: 1. [G] 段：网络商家追踪个人行为并实时为每个人调整价格或提示。2. [B] 段：选择过多时买家依赖可被刻意利用的心理捷径。3. [A] 段：从货架布局到音乐，整个零售环境都为促成计划外购买而设计。4. [H] 段：仅仅理解这些手法就给了购物者抵抗的部分能力。5. [D] 段：同一商品因周围对比物不同而显得便宜或昂贵。6. [F] 段：不确定的顾客信任大众，而这种倾向可被伪造评论操纵。7. [C] 段：购买常由品牌附带的情感而非功能驱动。8. [E] 段：暗示库存将尽会把轻微兴趣变成急切渴望。9. [A] 段：研究购买动机已成为应用心理学中高利润的分支。10. [C] 段：企业重金把产品与友情或成功等情感相连。

sets.passage:
[A] Not long ago privacy meant little more than drawing the curtains and lowering one's voice. A person could reasonably assume that what happened in a kitchen or a café left no permanent trace. That assumption has quietly collapsed, for the ordinary business of modern life now sheds a continuous stream of data that lingers long after the moment has passed.
[B] The richest source of this exposure is the device we carry everywhere. A smartphone logs where its owner travels, whom they contact, what they search at midnight, and how long they pause on each image. Bundled together, these fragments compose a portrait more candid than any its subject would knowingly volunteer, assembled without a single deliberate confession.
[C] Much of this surrender is dressed up as agreement. Before using a service we tap accept on documents so long and so dense that almost nobody reads them, consenting in name to terms we have not grasped in substance. Whether such a ritual deserves to be called genuine consent has become a pointed question for lawyers and ethicists alike.
[D] A further shift is that information once scattered now converges. Records that formerly sat in separate, dusty files, of purchases, journeys, and ailments, can be fused into a single profile by parties the individual never meets. The danger lies less in any one detail than in the revealing mosaic that emerges when the pieces are joined.
[E] Surveillance, moreover, no longer flows in one direction. Citizens film officials, neighbours photograph one another, and doorbell cameras capture the street, so that watching has become a habit shared by all rather than a power held by the few. This diffusion brings a certain accountability, yet it also thickens the atmosphere of mutual observation in which everyone now lives.
[F] Cultures, notably, disagree about where the lines should fall. In some societies the state is trusted to hold vast records in exchange for safety and convenience, while in others such accumulation provokes deep suspicion. What counts as an intolerable intrusion in one capital may pass for prudent administration in another, complicating any hope of a single global standard.
[G] Younger generations, raised online, appear to draw the boundary differently again. Many cheerfully broadcast details their elders would have guarded, yet they also prove fierce about controlling who sees what, and when. Their attitude suggests not the death of privacy but its reinvention as something negotiated, contextual, and selectively performed.
[H] What endures through all this flux is a simple need: the ability to decide, within limits, what others may know about us. Technology has not abolished that need but scattered the choices across a thousand invisible transactions. Reclaiming it will require not nostalgia for closed curtains but new tools, laws, and habits fit for a world that never forgets.
statements: Younger people share freely yet insist on controlling exactly who sees what and when.
The smartphone we carry constantly assembles an intimate portrait from countless small traces.
Modern privacy debate centres on a lasting need to govern what others may learn about us.
Different cultures disagree sharply over how much record-keeping is acceptable.
We routinely tap agreement to terms so dense that almost no one truly understands them.
Watching now runs in many directions as ordinary people film officials and each other.
Privacy once meant little more than closing curtains and speaking quietly.
Separate records of purchases, travel, and health can be merged into one revealing profile.
The true danger is the composite picture that forms once isolated details are fused together.
Everyday modern activity leaves a continuous data trail that outlasts the moment.
sets.explain_zh: 1. [G] 段：年轻人乐于分享却坚持掌控谁在何时看到什么。2. [B] 段：随身手机用无数细微痕迹拼出私密画像。3. [H] 段：现代隐私争论的核心是持续存在的“掌控他人可知信息”之需。4. [F] 段：不同文化对可接受的记录留存程度分歧很大。5. [C] 段：我们惯常点“同意”于几乎无人真正读懂的密集条款。6. [E] 段：监视已多向流动，普通人拍摄官员与彼此。7. [A] 段：过去隐私不过是拉上窗帘、压低嗓音。8. [D] 段：购物、出行、健康的分散记录可合并为一份揭露性档案。9. [D] 段：真正的危险在于零散细节融合后所形成的整体图景。10. [A] 段：现代日常活动留下超越当下的持续数据痕迹。

sets.passage:
[A] For more than a century the world ran chiefly on fuels dug or pumped from the ground, and the arrangement felt as permanent as gravity. That era is now visibly ending, not because the planet has run short of coal or oil, but because the economics of capturing energy from sun and wind have shifted with a speed almost nobody foresaw.
[B] The decisive change has been the collapse of cost. The price of generating a unit of electricity from sunlight has fallen so steeply over two decades that in many regions it now undercuts the cheapest fossil alternative. What was once an idealist's expensive gesture has, quietly and almost suddenly, become the hard-headed choice of accountants.
[C] Yet sun and wind share an awkward flaw: they keep their own schedule. Clouds gather and breezes drop without regard for the evening surge in demand, so a grid leaning heavily on them must somehow store surplus power for the hours when nature withholds it. The race to build cheap, vast batteries has therefore become as urgent as the race to build the panels themselves.
[D] A second obstacle is the grid itself. The wires strung across most countries were designed to carry power outward from a few large plants, not to gather dribbles from millions of rooftops and distant wind farms. Rewiring this aged network to handle many small, scattered, and fluctuating sources is a costly and unglamorous task that headlines rarely celebrate.
[E] The transition also redraws the map of advantage. Nations that imported fuel for generations may suddenly harvest their own sun or wind, loosening old dependencies, while regions that grew rich selling oil face an uncertain future. Energy, long a source of leverage between states, is being quietly reshuffled into a new and unfamiliar pattern of winners and losers.
[F] Employment forms another strand of the story. Mines and rigs that once anchored whole towns are winding down, even as factories making turbines and crews installing panels multiply. The net tally of jobs may well rise, yet that statistic offers cold comfort to a community whose particular livelihood is the one being phased out.
[G] Government policy remains the hinge on which the pace turns. Where leaders tax pollution, fund research, or guarantee a market for clean power, investment floods in; where signals waver, capital hesitates and waits. The technology may be ready, but its speed of adoption still depends heavily on the rules that politicians choose to write.
[H] Taken together, these threads point to a transformation that is no longer a question of if but of how fast and how fairly. Sunlight and wind will increasingly power the world; the open issues are whether the change arrives in time to ease the climate, and whether its costs and rewards are shared with any sense of justice.
statements: The pace of adoption hinges on whether governments tax pollution and guarantee markets for clean power.
The end of the fossil era stems not from scarcity but from a startling shift in the cost of renewables.
Old fuel importers may gain new independence while oil-rich regions face uncertainty.
Solar electricity has grown so cheap that it now beats the cheapest fossil option in many places.
Because sun and wind are intermittent, building affordable large-scale storage has become urgent.
Declining mines hurt specific towns even if total employment across the sector rises.
The remaining questions are about speed and fairness rather than whether the shift will happen.
Existing grids were built to send power outward from few large plants, not gather it from scattered sources.
Sunlight and wind keep their own schedule, ignoring the timing of peak demand.
Power has long given states leverage, and that balance is being quietly rearranged.
sets.explain_zh: 1. [G] 段：采用速度取决于政府是否对污染征税并保障清洁能源市场。2. [A] 段：化石时代终结源于可再生能源成本的惊人转变而非资源枯竭。3. [E] 段：旧燃料进口国或获新独立，而产油区面临不确定。4. [B] 段：太阳能电价已低到在许多地区胜过最便宜的化石选项。5. [C] 段：因风光间歇，建造廉价大规模储能已成当务之急。6. [F] 段：即便行业总就业上升，衰落的矿区仍受具体打击。7. [H] 段：余下问题是速度与公平，而非转变是否发生。8. [D] 段：现有电网为从少数大厂向外送电而建，非汇集分散电源。9. [C] 段：风光自有节奏，无视用电高峰时段。10. [E] 段：能源长期赋予国家筹码，如今这一格局正被悄然重排。

sets.passage:
[A] The image is familiar from a thousand anxious headlines: a tireless robot arm welding where a worker once stood, a self-checkout swallowing the role of a cashier. Automation, the steady substitution of machines for human effort, has stirred both wonder and dread since the first power loom, and the latest wave reaches into offices once thought immune.
[B] History offers a complicated reassurance. Earlier mechanisations destroyed familiar trades yet, over time, generated unforeseen new ones, so that fears of permanent mass idleness repeatedly proved overblown. Optimists lean on this record, arguing that the displaced will once again migrate to occupations we cannot yet imagine, just as ploughmen's grandchildren became programmers.
[C] Sceptics, however, detect a difference in the present surge. Where past machines mainly replaced muscle, today's systems increasingly mimic judgement, drafting documents, diagnosing faults, and reading scans. If machines encroach on cognitive work as well as manual, the comforting escape routes of the past, into thinking jobs, may grow narrower than the historical analogy suggests.
[D] A subtler worry concerns not the number of jobs but their distribution. Automation tends to hollow out the middle, sparing both the lowest-paid tasks that are cheap to leave human and the highest that demand rare creativity, while devouring the steady clerical and routine roles in between. The result is a workforce pulled toward two poles, with the secure middle rung increasingly missing.
[E] The disruption also lands unevenly across places. A town built around a single factory may be devastated when its line is automated, even as prosperity swells in distant hubs of design and finance. Aggregate national figures can stay healthy while particular communities are quietly gutted, a gap that averages conveniently conceal.
[F] Beyond wages lies a question of meaning. For many people work supplies not merely income but structure, dignity, and a sense of contribution, so that losing a job can wound an identity as much as a bank balance. A future of abundant goods produced by machines might still leave large numbers feeling adrift and superfluous.
[G] Various remedies are debated. Some urge heavy investment in retraining so workers can keep pace; others propose sharing the machines' bounty through new forms of income support; still others insist that fresh demand will, as ever, conjure fresh employment. Each prescription rests on a different reading of how deep and how swift the coming change will be.
[H] What seems clear is that the outcome is not dictated by the technology alone. Whether automation liberates people for richer pursuits or strands them in insecurity will depend on the choices societies make about education, taxation, and the sharing of gains. The machines pose the question; the answer remains stubbornly human.
statements: Work provides dignity and identity, so its loss can wound more than just income.
Past mechanisation eliminated old trades but eventually created unforeseen new ones.
Whether automation liberates or strands people depends on social choices, not technology alone.
Today's machines increasingly imitate judgement, narrowing the old escape into thinking jobs.
National averages can look healthy while specific automated towns are quietly devastated.
Proposed responses range from retraining to income support to faith in new demand.
Automation tends to erode middle-tier routine roles while sparing the lowest and highest.
The substitution of machines for human effort has provoked awe and fear since the power loom.
Optimists believe the displaced will migrate to occupations not yet imaginable.
Abundant machine-made goods could still leave many feeling purposeless and surplus.
sets.explain_zh: 1. [F] 段：工作提供尊严与认同，失去它的伤害不止于收入。2. [B] 段：过去机械化消灭旧行当却最终催生意料之外的新行当。3. [H] 段：自动化是解放还是搁浅取决于社会选择而非技术本身。4. [C] 段：当今机器日益模仿判断，收窄通往思维型工作的旧出路。5. [E] 段：全国平均值看似健康，特定被自动化的城镇却被悄然摧毁。6. [G] 段：应对方案从再培训到收入支持再到对新需求的信心。7. [D] 段：自动化侵蚀中层常规岗位，却放过最低与最高端。8. [A] 段：自动力织机以来，以机器替代人力即引发惊叹与恐惧。9. [B] 段：乐观者相信被取代者会转向尚无法想象的职业。10. [F] 段：机器制造的丰裕仍可能让许多人感到无用与多余。

sets.passage:
[A] For centuries the pursuit of a good life was the province of priests and philosophers, who reasoned about virtue and contemplation with little help from data. Only recently have researchers begun to treat happiness as a phenomenon that can be measured, compared, and dissected, turning a timeless mystery into a subject for cautious experiment.
[B] One of the field's earliest and most stubborn findings concerns money. Beyond the point where basic needs are met, additional wealth buys surprisingly little extra contentment, for human expectations rise to swallow each gain. A raise that thrills for a month soon feels like the new normal, leaving the recipient reaching, as before, for the next rung.
[C] Relationships, by contrast, prove a far steadier source of well-being. Long-running studies that follow the same people across decades report, with unusual consistency, that the warmth and depth of one's connections predict a flourishing old age better than fame, income, or even physical health measured in youth. We are, it seems, social creatures to the marrow.
[D] The mind's own machinery, however, conspires against lasting joy. Humans adapt swiftly to whatever they acquire, so the thrill of a new house or gadget fades as the brain recalibrates to treat the novel as ordinary. This relentless adjustment, useful for survival, ensures that pleasures grounded in possession tend to be fleeting.
[E] Curiously, giving often outperforms getting. Experiments find that people instructed to spend a small sum on someone else report a sharper lift in mood than those told to spend it on themselves. The act of contributing to another's welfare appears to satisfy a need that mere acquisition cannot reach.
[F] Attention, too, shapes contentment more than circumstance. A mind that wanders into rumination about past slights or future threats tends to suffer, whereas one absorbed in the present task, however humble, reports greater ease. Training the spotlight of attention, through practices such as meditation, can therefore lift mood independently of any change in one's situation.
[G] Culture frames the whole inquiry, for the very meaning of a good life varies across societies. Where one tradition prizes cheerful individual achievement, another esteems calm harmony with family and community, so that questionnaires designed in one place may misread the well-being of people elsewhere. Happiness, it turns out, wears different faces.
[H] None of this reduces to a tidy formula, yet a loose consensus has emerged. Lasting contentment owes less to acquiring possessions than to nurturing bonds, helping others, and steering attention wisely, lessons the old philosophers half-knew and the new science is steadily confirming with evidence.
statements: Spending a small amount on others tends to lift mood more than spending it on oneself.
Beyond meeting basic needs, extra wealth adds little contentment as expectations keep rising.
Where attention dwells matters more than circumstance, and it can be trained to improve mood.
Researchers have only lately begun to measure and analyse happiness rather than merely reason about it.
Decades-long studies show the quality of relationships predicts a flourishing old age best.
The loose consensus favours bonds, generosity, and managed attention over possessions.
The meaning of a good life differs across cultures, so one society's survey may misjudge another.
Humans quickly adapt to new acquisitions, so pleasures rooted in owning things fade.
Long-running observation of the same individuals underlies the finding about connection.
The brain's habit of treating the novel as ordinary makes possession-based joy short-lived.
sets.explain_zh: 1. [E] 段：把小钱花在他人身上比花在自己身上更能提升心情。2. [B] 段：满足基本需求后，财富因期望不断攀升而几乎不增添满足。3. [F] 段：注意力所在比处境更重要，且可通过训练改善心情。4. [A] 段：研究者近来才开始测量分析幸福，而非仅作推理。5. [C] 段：数十年追踪显示关系质量最能预测晚年的幸福。6. [H] 段：松散共识更看重联结、慷慨与对注意力的管理而非占有。7. [G] 段：美好生活的含义因文化而异，一地问卷可能误判他地。8. [D] 段：人迅速适应新得之物，使基于占有的快乐褪色。9. [C] 段：关于联结的发现以对同一群人的长期观察为基础。10. [D] 段：大脑把新奇当作平常的习惯使占有式快乐短暂。

sets.passage:
[A] Stroll through any large city and the evidence is everywhere: a Japanese dish served in a Brazilian kitchen, an American film dubbed into a dozen tongues, a pop song whose verses leap between languages. Culture, once tethered firmly to a place, now travels with astonishing ease, blending and recombining in ways that would have bewildered earlier generations.
[B] The engine of this mixing is connection. Cheap flights move people across oceans for work or pleasure, while screens carry images and sounds instantly to the remotest village. A teenager in a mountain town may now follow the same stars, mimic the same dances, and crave the same brands as a peer in a faraway metropolis, drawing on a shared global stream.
[C] To admirers this exchange is a feast. Exposure to unfamiliar food, music, and ideas, they argue, widens sympathy and dissolves the suspicion that distance breeds. A young person who grows up enjoying the art of distant peoples may find it harder, later, to regard those peoples as alien or to wish them harm.
[D] Critics see a graver cost. As a handful of dominant industries broadcast their products worldwide, local songs, dialects, and crafts struggle to compete, and some quietly vanish. What is sold as a colourful global menu can, on closer inspection, reveal the same few flavours repeated, a uniformity masquerading as variety.
[E] Yet the flow seldom runs in only one direction. Communities on the receiving end rarely swallow foreign goods whole; instead they adapt, parody, and reinvent them, stitching imported elements into something distinctly their own. A borrowed rhythm acquires a local accent, and a foreign festival is reshaped to fit native meanings.
[F] Commerce complicates any clean judgement. Tradition has itself become a product, packaged and sold to travellers hungry for the authentic, so that a ritual once performed for the gods may now be staged for paying audiences. Whether this commercial attention preserves a heritage or hollows it out is fiercely disputed among those who study it.
[G] Politics, too, intrudes. Some governments embrace the inflow as proof of openness, while others raise barriers, fearing that imported values will erode cherished customs or loosen their grip on a restless population. The very same film may be celebrated in one capital and quietly banned in another, depending on what its rulers fear.
[H] In the end the picture resists a single verdict. The globalisation of culture neither simply enriches nor merely flattens; it does both at once, opening minds while threatening difference. Its ultimate shape will be decided less by abstract forces than by the countless small choices of people deciding what to keep, what to borrow, and what to let go.
statements: Heritage itself has become merchandise sold to tourists seeking authenticity.
Easy travel and instant screens are the main drivers of cultural mixing.
Culture, once tied to a single place, now moves and recombines with remarkable ease.
Receiving communities rarely accept imports whole, instead reshaping them into something local.
The outcome will be set by countless small human choices about what to keep or borrow.
Admirers claim exposure to other cultures widens sympathy and reduces hostility.
Some governments welcome the inflow while others block it, fearing eroded customs.
Critics warn that a few dominant industries crowd out local songs, dialects, and crafts.
A teenager in a remote town now shares the same stars and brands as a distant city peer.
What looks like a varied global menu may actually repeat the same few flavours.
sets.explain_zh: 1. [F] 段：遗产本身已成为卖给寻求“正宗”游客的商品。2. [B] 段：廉价旅行与即时屏幕是文化混融的主要动力。3. [A] 段：文化曾系于一地，如今以惊人之易流动并重组。4. [E] 段：接收方很少全盘照收，而是将舶来品重塑为本地之物。5. [H] 段：最终结果由人们关于取舍借用的无数小选择决定。6. [C] 段：仰慕者称接触他文化能拓宽同理、减少敌意。7. [G] 段：一些政府欢迎流入，另一些则因怕侵蚀习俗而设障。8. [D] 段：批评者警告少数主导产业挤压本地歌谣、方言与工艺。9. [B] 段：偏远小镇少年如今与远方都市同侪共享同样明星与品牌。10. [D] 段：看似多样的全球菜单实则可能重复同样几种口味。

sets.passage:
[A] Quietly, almost imperceptibly, the human population is growing older. Falling birth rates and lengthening lives are together tilting the age structure of nation after nation, so that for the first time in history the old will soon outnumber the young across much of the world. This demographic turn, gradual yet relentless, will reshape economies and families in ways societies have barely begun to confront.
[B] The most discussed consequence is fiscal. Pension and health systems were largely designed for an era of many workers supporting few retirees, and that ratio is now inverting. As the contributing base shrinks while claims multiply, governments face the unwelcome arithmetic of higher taxes, later retirement, or thinner benefits, none of which wins applause at the ballot box.
[C] Labour markets feel the strain differently. With fewer young entrants, employers in many fields confront a dwindling pool of recruits, and skills painstakingly built over decades threaten to walk out the door at retirement. Some firms respond by tempting experienced staff to stay on, redesigning roles to suit older bodies and the wisdom that accompanies grey hair.
[D] Caregiving poses a quieter crisis. As people live longer, more reach years of frailty requiring daily help, yet the smaller younger generation is hard pressed to supply it. The burden often falls on middle-aged women juggling jobs and dependents, or on a strained corps of paid carers whose work remains undervalued and poorly compensated.
[E] Health itself takes on a new shape. Medicine increasingly contends not with the swift infectious killers of the past but with the slow, chronic conditions of long life, ailments that are managed rather than cured. Keeping an ageing population not merely alive but active becomes the central challenge, shifting the goal from longevity to vitality.
[F] There are, however, overlooked opportunities. Older citizens are not a uniform mass of dependence; many remain productive, mentoring the young, volunteering, and spending savings in ways that sustain whole industries. A society that dismisses them as a burden squanders a reservoir of experience and demand that could, if tapped, soften the very strains it fears.
[G] Migration enters the debate as a partial remedy. Younger newcomers can replenish a thinning workforce and prop up the systems an ageing native population cannot sustain alone, yet such inflows stir anxieties about identity and cohesion. The policy thus offers relief with one hand while igniting controversy with the other.
[H] Ultimately the ageing of humanity demands not panic but reinvention. The institutions of work, care, and retirement were built for a younger world and must be redrawn for an older one. Whether this transition becomes a slow-motion crisis or a chance to design more humane arrangements depends on the imagination societies bring to it now.
statements: Younger immigrants can refill a shrinking workforce but stir worries about identity.
Pension and health systems built for many workers per retiree now face an inverted ratio.
Older people are not uniformly dependent; many remain productive and sustain industries.
Falling birth rates and longer lives mean the old will soon outnumber the young in much of the world.
Longer life shifts medicine toward managing slow chronic conditions rather than curing swift ones.
Meeting the transition well requires redesigning institutions of work, care, and retirement.
With fewer young recruits, employers risk losing hard-won skills as veterans retire.
The duty of daily care for the frail often falls on middle-aged women or underpaid carers.
Governments face unpopular choices among higher taxes, later retirement, or smaller benefits.
A society that treats its elderly only as a burden wastes their experience and spending power.
sets.explain_zh: 1. [G] 段：年轻移民可补充收缩的劳动力却引发身份焦虑。2. [B] 段：为多劳动者养少退休者而建的养老与医疗制度面临比例倒置。3. [F] 段：老年人并非一律依赖，许多仍有产出并支撑产业。4. [A] 段：出生率下降与寿命延长使老年人很快在世界多地超过年轻人。5. [E] 段：长寿使医学转向管理缓慢慢性病而非治愈急性病。6. [H] 段：良好渡过转型需要重新设计工作、照护与退休制度。7. [C] 段：年轻新人减少使雇主面临老将退休带走技能的风险。8. [D] 段：照护体弱者的日常重担常落在中年女性或低薪护工身上。9. [B] 段：政府面临加税、延退或减福利等不受欢迎的选择。10. [F] 段：把老人仅当负担的社会浪费了其经验与消费力。
